title: C**tgate

jokes.text: It is possible some banks may require massive capital infusions. / That's the literal definition of bailing something out.
jokes.setup: Mike insists there will be no bailouts while describing something that is unmistakably a bailout.
jokes.punchline: An advisor immediately and flatly points out the semantic contradiction.
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep political doublespeak joke — the humor is in the barefaced spinning of language and the instantaneous debunking. Sharp economy and good structure but not especially novel in political satire.

jokes.text: So, next up on the hit parade, we get to decide which banks to bail out.
jokes.setup: Mike has just re-declared there will be no bailouts.
jokes.punchline: The advisor cheerfully pivots to discussing which bailouts to do, treating the contradiction as a settled, unremarkable matter.
jokes.explanation: Rapid callback that escalates the doublespeak joke. The breezy 'hit parade' phrasing undercuts the gravity of a financial crisis. Works as a quick double-punch with joke 1.

jokes.text: Well, I hope to fiscal fuck the fed has a trillion dollars stuffed in a mattress somewhere.
jokes.setup: The meeting is shifting to the serious matter of which banks need bailing out.
jokes.punchline: Selina's profane, exasperated wishful thinking — 'fiscal fuck' as an invented oath — undercuts the gravity of the crisis with pure id.
jokes.explanation: The invented portmanteau oath 'fiscal fuck' is Veep writing at its best — highly specific to the political-financial context, deeply character-integrated (only Selina would say this), and economical. The 'mattress' image completes the absurdist picture with perfect brevity. A strong candidate for episode's most quotable line.

jokes.text: And if we do nothing? / Go to the ATM Monday and dust will come out.
jokes.setup: Selina asks what happens if they fail to act on the bank crisis.
jokes.punchline: The advisor's blunt, vivid image of ATMs dispensing dust instead of cash conveys total economic collapse in a single absurd picture.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy — the entire catastrophic consequence of a banking collapse rendered in one concrete, absurd image. Almost haiku-like in efficiency. The deadpan delivery context makes it funnier. Just shy of an 8 on craft because it's a one-liner without much character specificity.

jokes.text: My pick is EM Wheelright. / You know what, Tom? I think... oh, yes. What he just said.
jokes.setup: Tom makes a bank recommendation that coincides with Selina's romantic interest. Selina is about to object.
jokes.punchline: Selina catches herself mid-objection and immediately — transparently — endorses Tom's choice because it lets her bail out her boyfriend's bank.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully character-integrated — Selina's self-serving pivot mid-sentence is a perfect snapshot of her opportunism. The pause before 'oh, yes. What he just said' does a lot of comedic work in very few words.

jokes.text: Because that's gonna look like you care more about your boyfriend than you do the economy, normal people, and everything else you're supposed to care about. / AIDS for example. / Thanks, Kent's autism.
jokes.setup: Amy lists the things Selina is supposed to care about more than her boyfriend's bank.
jokes.punchline: Kent adds 'AIDS for example' as a specific, absurdly clinical illustration. A third voice (likely another staffer) immediately roasts Kent's interjection as coming from his 'autism.'
jokes.explanation: Two-beat joke with an unexpected third layer. Kent's 'AIDS for example' is delightfully specific and clinical in exactly the wrong context. The immediate response of 'Thanks, Kent's autism' is a perfect Veep rapid-fire roast — edgy but character-earned. The darkness is justified by the show's satirical register.

jokes.text: At least when Truman made the decision to drop the bomb, he wasn't fucking anyone in Hiroshima. / That we know of.
jokes.setup: Amy uses a historical analogy to illustrate the problem of Selina's conflict of interest in the bank decision.
jokes.punchline: A quick, dark addendum — 'That we know of' — undercuts the moral clarity of the historical reference with a conspiratorial aside.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's sharpest jokes. Amy's setup is already darkly funny — the Hiroshima comparison as a conflict-of-interest metaphor is original and perfectly structured. The four-word addendum 'That we know of' is a masterclass in comic economy, simultaneously landing a conspiracy joke, a historical joke, and keeping the dark energy alive. Highly quotable, strong rewatch value.

jokes.text: Jonah Ryan campaign ad — 'I grew up right here in the awesome state of New Hampshire. The Granite State of the United States.'
jokes.setup: Jonah's campaign ad is presented as political outreach.
jokes.punchline: The ad is painfully generic and hollow — 'awesome state,' redundant 'Granite State of the United States' — perfectly conveying Jonah's complete lack of substance.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the ad's utter vacuity as satire of political advertising, combined with Jonah's total lack of self-awareness. Solid but the humor is somewhat expected given the show's setup of Jonah as an incompetent.

jokes.text: That wood he's chopping, it's not gonna burn right. / To burn... the wood is not going to burn.
jokes.setup: Focus group members are asked what they think of Jonah's campaign ad.
jokes.punchline: A focus group participant's only complaint is about the practical suitability of the firewood being chopped — not Jonah's politics, personality, or competence.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of the complaint — wood burning — is genuinely funny as a left-field focus group observation. It satirizes how voters evaluate candidates on irrelevant criteria. The repetition ('to burn... the wood is not going to burn') adds a beautifully confused pedantic note.

jokes.text: His head is too big for his body. But then sometimes his body is too big for his head. / He's the wrong shape.
jokes.setup: Focus group continues evaluating Jonah's ad.
jokes.punchline: Multiple participants critique Jonah's physical proportions with escalating logical confusion — he's simultaneously too big and too small — concluding with the simple verdict 'He's the wrong shape.'
jokes.explanation: The escalating absurdity of the proportionality critique, capped by the wonderfully reductive 'He's the wrong shape,' is excellent comedy. Veep has always made Jonah's gangly physicality a running gag, and this focus group sequence exploits it perfectly through the lens of bewildered ordinary citizens.

jokes.text: I like the kid. / Like the kid in the ad, the little boy. / But I did not like that he was next to that guy. / I was like, 'Run, kid.'
jokes.setup: Asked for anything positive about the ad, focus group members cite the child featured in it.
jokes.punchline: The child's proximity to Jonah triggers an instinctive warning — 'Run, kid' — suggesting Jonah reads as threatening even to strangers who know nothing about him.
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly constructed: the setup dangles false hope ('I like the kid'), deflates it ('I did not like that he was next to that guy'), then delivers a vivid, instinctive reaction ('Run, kid') that functions both as a roast of Jonah and a sharp satire of focus-group dynamics. Highly quotable and perfectly economical.

jokes.text: Oh, surprise, surprise. Look who's here. / Do you morons really not understand that this is a two-way mirror? / Seriously? Are you shocked by that technology?
jokes.setup: Jonah, watching the focus group through a two-way mirror, storms in to confront them.
jokes.punchline: Jonah is so enraged he cannot resist revealing himself, completely undermining the purpose of the focus group, while condescendingly insulting participants about technology they clearly understand.
jokes.explanation: Peak Jonah character comedy — his inability to control himself destroys the exercise while he tries to assert dominance. 'Are you shocked by that technology?' is a perfect Jonah line: condescending, misplaced, self-defeating. Character-integrated at a high level.

jokes.text: I work in the fucking West Wing, you Pepperidge Farm ad motherfuckers.
jokes.setup: Jonah continues his meltdown at the focus group participants.
jokes.punchline: The insult 'Pepperidge Farm ad motherfuckers' — evoking wholesome, rural, nostalgic imagery — is deployed as a profane attack on regular New Hampshire voters, perfectly capturing Jonah's contemptuous disconnect from constituents he's trying to court.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The collision of 'Pepperidge Farm' (wholesome, grandmotherly brand) with 'motherfuckers' is a linguistically original and perfectly Jonah-specific insult. It simultaneously reveals his contempt for voters, his warped self-image as a Washington elite, and his catastrophic judgment. The West Wing name-drop as a credential while berating his own constituents is political satire at its sharpest. Genuinely exceptional economy and character integration — comparable to the 8.0 anchor benchmark, and arguably above it.

jokes.text: Fuck you. / Watch your mouth. / Sit your fucking mom jeans ass down, dude. / You've got to learn to control your fucking temper!
jokes.setup: Jonah's meltdown escalates into a full confrontation with focus group members.
jokes.punchline: A focus group member tells Jonah to control his temper — with profanity — while Jonah simultaneously tells them to 'sit their mom jeans ass down,' creating a beautiful mirror of mutual rage.
jokes.explanation: The escalation of mutual profanity is funny, and 'mom jeans ass' is a characteristically Jonah insult. The joke is somewhat diffused by being a continuation of the prior beat rather than a distinct punchline, but the focus group member out-Jonahing Jonah ('control your fucking temper!') is a nice structural reversal.

jokes.text: This is beyond language. / I know the world of focus groups. / I think maybe we picked the wrong candidate.
jokes.setup: Jonah has just destroyed the focus group with his meltdown.
jokes.punchline: The campaign manager's clinical, understated assessment — 'I think maybe we picked the wrong candidate' — delivered as a measured professional conclusion after an absolute catastrophe, is a textbook deadpan punchline.
jokes.explanation: The 'maybe' is doing enormous comedic work — it's the understatement of the century after what just happened. Classic Veep deadpan punctuation of a cringe escalation sequence.

jokes.text: Uh-huh. How is Catherine there and also here?
jokes.setup: Selina is watching footage on a tablet where Catherine is filming documentary B-roll of the White House.
jokes.punchline: Selina has not noticed that her own daughter has been filming in the White House and is genuinely confused about her daughter's physical location, revealing her complete inattentiveness to Catherine.
jokes.explanation: A sharp little character joke — Selina is so disengaged from her daughter's existence that she hasn't even clocked that Catherine is physically present. Sets up the episode's Catherine subplot economically.

jokes.text: "Recession Has a New NAME: the Selina Slump"? / No. Gary, you have the machine? / Listen to this. / 'In a further sign of low morale'... / No, you've got to read it properly. / Nope, that's not it. / You've got to spin it. / Here we go. Why does it keep doing the turning?
jokes.setup: Selina wants to demonstrate a damaging Politico article about her being called a cunt, using a tablet that Gary is struggling to operate.
jokes.punchline: The increasingly frantic search through the tablet — 'Why does it keep doing the turning?' — perfectly undercuts the gravity of the moment as Gary's technological incompetence derails Selina's outrage.
jokes.explanation: Gary's hapless technological struggle is good character comedy and a nice tonal deflation of Selina's outrage. 'Why does it keep doing the turning?' is a funny line but the bit runs slightly long before the payoff.

jokes.text: "A high-level West Wing staffer was recently overheard calling the president the C word." / Can you believe this shit? / Yeah, this is broken, by the way.
jokes.setup: After the struggle with the tablet, Selina finally reads the damaging article about being called a cunt.
jokes.punchline: Gary's only contribution after the dramatic revelation is to note that the tablet is broken — completely failing to engage with the actual crisis.
jokes.explanation: Gary's deflating postscript ('this is broken, by the way') is a perfect Gary moment — his priorities are the device, not Selina's humiliation. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: We need a Jonah whisperer, except somebody who's gonna, like, yell in his face and call him stupid. / Oh, we need Dan Egan.
jokes.setup: The team discusses who can manage Jonah's campaign.
jokes.punchline: The description of the needed person — someone who yells and calls Jonah stupid — is so perfectly Dan that a staffer immediately names him, confirming Dan's entire character in one beat.
jokes.explanation: Tight, character-specific, structurally elegant. The setup describes a job description that is essentially Dan Egan's personality, and the immediate recognition ('Oh, we need Dan Egan') is a perfect sitcom revelation beat. Economical and strongly character-integrated for both Selina and Dan.

jokes.text: Well, are you doing it? / You're just sitting here farting into my couch.
jokes.setup: Ben claims he has interviews lined up for the Jonah campaign manager job.
jokes.punchline: Selina points out he is literally just sitting on the couch doing nothing, with the extraordinarily specific and degrading image of 'farting into my couch.'
jokes.explanation: Selina's profane specificity is one of her best qualities as a comedy character. 'Farting into my couch' as an image of professional uselessness is vivid, unexpected, and perfectly contemptuous. Strong Selina character integration.

jokes.text: I want you to find out who called me a cunt.
jokes.setup: Selina privately asks Amy to investigate who leaked the story about her being called a cunt.
jokes.punchline: The blunt directness of Selina stating the C-word herself while directing the investigation into its use against her is deliciously ironic — she's reclaiming the word as her weapon while being simultaneously outraged by it.
jokes.explanation: More setup for the running Cuntgate gag than a fully developed joke, but Selina's blunt deployment of the word she's outraged about is characteristically sharp.

jokes.text: Hey, Dan. You want to go to lunch later at Hansong Korean Barbecue? / Come on, I've got a Groupon, expires Monday. / 50 bucks for $100 worth of food. / It's in Annandale, Mike. No one from DC goes to fucking Annandale.
jokes.setup: Mike tries to cajole Dan into accepting the Jonah campaign job by suggesting lunch.
jokes.punchline: Dan's rejection of the Groupon invite focuses entirely on the suburban location ('fucking Annandale') as beneath a DC operative's dignity — not the Groupon itself, not the Korean barbecue, but the specific suburb.
jokes.explanation: Dan's Washington-elite snobbery about a Virginia suburb is very in-character. The Hansong/Groupon detail is a plant that pays off beautifully at the episode's end. Good setup for a later callback.

jokes.text: Hey, how about an $80 in-home massage for 40 bucks? / With release? / No. / Then what's the point?
jokes.setup: Mike continues scrolling through Groupons to tempt Dan.
jokes.punchline: Dan's dismissal of a massage Groupon on the grounds it doesn't include a 'happy ending' — delivered with total seriousness as a legitimate deal-breaker.
jokes.explanation: The joke itself isn't hugely original but the execution is tight. Dan's 'Then what's the point?' with its implication that a massage's only value is the sexual component is perfectly in character — his transactional worldview applied to leisure. The economy of four words as a punchline is excellent.

jokes.text: Jesus, Dan, you wouldn't know the smart move if it bent you over and fucked you with a Coke bottle.
jokes.setup: Mike tries to convince Dan to take the Jonah campaign job, arguing that it's strategically smart.
jokes.punchline: Mike's argument is delivered via an extraordinarily graphic and violent sexual metaphor — deeply incongruous coming from the usually hapless, mild-mannered Mike.
jokes.explanation: The shock of such a graphic image coming from Mike is the joke — his usual blandness makes the Coke bottle metaphor especially startling. 'Wouldn't know the smart move' is also a comedically unusual way to frame the metaphor, making the whole thing more original than a simple profanity-laced insult.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, Candi. The position's been filled. / Aw. / Thanks for coming in. / It's good seeing you again. / Mm-hmm.
jokes.setup: Dan interrupts Ben's interview with candidate Candi Caruso to speak with him privately.
jokes.punchline: Ben immediately fires Candi on Dan's behalf the moment Dan appears, without hesitation or discussion, making it obvious Dan has already been chosen. The 'It's good seeing you again / Mm-hmm' coda adds a frozen politeness to the dismissal.
jokes.explanation: The instantaneous dismissal of Candi is a clean joke about how the political world really operates — relationships over meritocracy. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Is this about the coffee pods I took from the kitchen? / We had a brunch and I took them.
jokes.setup: Amy approaches Mike to ask him about who called Selina a cunt, framing it as a serious matter.
jokes.punchline: Mike immediately assumes he's in trouble about stolen coffee pods and preemptively confesses to this minor crime, revealing his constant low-level guilt and anxiety.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy. Mike's guilty conscience about coffee pods — a petty crime he immediately elevates to confessional status — is a wonderfully specific character beat. 'We had a brunch and I took them' as a justification is perfectly Mike: slightly defensive, hopelessly mundane.

jokes.text: Okay, I'm sorry. I can't do this. She put me in charge of this whole stupid investigation and it was me. I called her a cunt. / You did? / I thought everyone knew it was me. / I was in the Senate building with Dan and I'm sure someone overheard me. / I shouted it into my phone on the Acela Quiet Car.
jokes.setup: Mike is being questioned by Amy about the cunt leak.
jokes.punchline: Mike confesses immediately — but the escalating details of HOW he did it are extraordinary: he shouted it on a train's Quiet Car, into his phone, in the Senate building. The confession is worse than the crime.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's first truly exceptional joke sequence. The escalation structure is impeccable: Mike breaks almost immediately (funny), then the how compounds the why: he 'shouted it into his phone' (worse), 'on the Acela Quiet Car' (specifically worse — the one place designed for silence). Each detail is a fresh laugh. Strong character integration for Mike's hapless, self-defeating persona. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor joke in quality.

jokes.text: I've made my decision. We are going to bail out Charlie's bank. I don't care what hits we take. / Bravo, Madam President. / That's my final solution. / 'Who Wants to be a Millionaire?' / Do you get it? Yeah.
jokes.setup: Selina makes a bold, politically courageous decision and declares it her 'final solution.'
jokes.punchline: A staffer immediately catches the Holocaust-adjacent phrasing, nervously references 'Who Wants to be a Millionaire' (a lifeline — asking the audience) as a coded signal, then immediately asks if Selina gets it. She clearly doesn't.
jokes.explanation: The accidental deployment of 'final solution' is a classic dark Veep joke, and the nervous staffer's elaborate code-speak to flag it — referencing a game show's 'ask the audience' lifeline — is original and funny. The silent confirmation that Selina doesn't get it is the real punchline.

jokes.text: Are you even getting any of this? / Getting what? / I was looking thoughtfully out the window. / Some straight up JFK level shit. / I was just getting some B-roll of the fax machine.
jokes.setup: Catherine is filming a documentary in the White House and Selina tries to perform a presidential moment.
jokes.punchline: Selina poses at the window doing her 'JFK level shit' — only to learn Catherine is filming the fax machine. Selina's performative gravitas is entirely wasted on mundane equipment.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character joke on both Selina and Catherine. Selina's self-conscious performance of presidential gravitas ('JFK level shit') being aimed at a fax machine is a perfect comic deflation. The mother-daughter dynamic — Catherine's indifference to Selina's vanity — is well-integrated.

jokes.text: Sir, as your director of communications, I need to tell you that we just got a letter from the band Rush. / Really? / They said that we can't use the song 'Working Man' anymore because they find you odious.
jokes.setup: Richard informs Jonah of a cease-and-desist from the band Rush.
jokes.punchline: Rush's objection isn't copyright or commercial concerns — they 'find you odious.' A rock band is actively expressing personal disgust with Jonah as a human being.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'odious' as the word Rush chose is funny — it's formal and Victorian but also unambiguous. The joke lands because it personalizes what is usually a legal/commercial dispute into a moral rejection. First of a running gag about bands rejecting Jonah.

jokes.text: You know what? I play what I want. Copyright shit went out with the Zune.
jokes.setup: Jonah responds to Rush's cease-and-desist.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's legal theory — copyright law is obsolete because the Zune media player (a notoriously failed Microsoft product) no longer exists — is spectacularly wrong and reveals his complete ignorance while attempting to sound cutting-edge.
jokes.explanation: An exceptional line. 'Copyright shit went out with the Zune' is perfect Jonah — overconfident, utterly wrong, deploying a failed tech product as a timestamp for cultural obsolescence. The Zune is such a specific and perfectly chosen symbol of irrelevance. Highly quotable, original, and character-specific in a way that no other character on this show could say this line. Clears the 8.0 anchor benchmark.

jokes.text: Whoa, I'm sorry. What are you doing here? / Hello, Jonah. Richard. / Hi, Catherine. / When you talk to me, you ruin the film. / Always a pleasure.
jokes.setup: Amy arrives at Jonah's campaign headquarters.
jokes.punchline: Catherine's documentarian instruction — 'When you talk to me, you ruin the film' — is a beautiful piece of absurdist logic: the film she's making is apparently ruined by acknowledgment of its maker. Richard's 'Always a pleasure' after being snubbed is deadpan gold.
jokes.explanation: Catherine's documentarian pretension played straight against Richard's gentle social grace makes for a fun quick beat. Not a big joke but well-executed.

jokes.text: From now on, Jonah, you will shut the fuck up and do exactly as I say. And if you listen to me instead of your only two brain cells busy butt-fucking each other somewhere in the vast expanses of your misshapen skull, then maybe, Jonah, you might have a chance at becoming the first mentally impaired Frankenstein's monster to ever win an American election.
jokes.setup: Amy announces she is taking over as campaign manager and lays out expectations.
jokes.punchline: Amy's extended anatomical insult — 'two brain cells busy butt-fucking each other in the vast expanses of your misshapen skull' — culminating in 'the first mentally impaired Frankenstein's monster to ever win an American election.'
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The construction is exceptional — starting with the demand for compliance, transitioning to a brain metaphor of extraordinary anatomical vulgarity, then landing on the perfectly specific insult 'mentally impaired Frankenstein's monster' as the aspirational ceiling for Jonah. Amy's fury channeled into precise, escalating invective is the show at its best. The extended form is justified because each clause is a fresh laugh. Strongly character-integrated for both Amy's volcanic competence and Jonah's pathetic incompetence.

jokes.text: And by the way, your banner makes it look like your name is Jon H. Ryan, so great job, communications department.
jokes.setup: Amy finishes her speech and surveys the campaign headquarters.
jokes.punchline: The banner font/spacing error has created 'Jon H. Ryan' — a visual gag that simultaneously roasts Richard's communications work and Jonah's general incompetence.
jokes.explanation: Sharp visual-to-dialogue joke. The banner error is funny as a detail of campaign incompetence, and 'great job, communications department' as sarcastic summary is well-timed. Strong rewatch bonus because viewers will look for the banner retroactively.

jokes.text: How do you look so good with all this stress? You're glowing. You actually look younger. What did you do? / Oh, my God, he noticed. / What? / Uh, I have a new facial moisturizer. / And a new soap.
jokes.setup: Charlie compliments Selina's appearance. Selina reacts with barely-contained joy.
jokes.punchline: Selina's ecstatic internal 'Oh, my God, he noticed' and then her and Gary's coordinated cover story — 'facial moisturizer / and a new soap' — reveals she has had cosmetic work (an eye job, confirmed later) and the whole room knows it while pretending not to.
jokes.explanation: A lovely cringe comedy sequence. Gary's 'Yeah, I'll just stand over here' then 'I'll just leave the room' confirms he knows the secret and is physically removing himself from the lie. Selina's 'Oh, my God, he noticed' is a perfectly vain, girlish, non-presidential beat. Strong character work across the board.

jokes.text: Gary. / Yeah, I'll just stand over here. / Actually, I think... / I'll just leave the room. / Yes. / Yeah.
jokes.setup: Charlie and Selina begin moving toward a private moment. Gary is present.
jokes.punchline: Gary's progressive self-removal — 'stand over here' then 'leave the room' — executed with complete awareness of his own superfluousness, is a perfect Gary beat.
jokes.explanation: Gary's socially aware awkwardness — knowing he should leave, starting to stay, then correcting himself — is a quietly funny character beat. His codependent desire to remain in Selina's orbit fighting his social awareness is all in 'Yeah, I'll just stand over here... I'll just leave the room.'

jokes.text: Do you want to just have sex? / Yeah. / Yeah. / It's got to be quick, though. I got wheels up at 9:30. / Ooh, that turns me on.
jokes.setup: After a brief, stilted 'let's talk about something else' moment, Selina and Charlie reach a conversational impasse.
jokes.punchline: Selina's blunt 'Do you want to just have sex?' cuts through the pretense; the joke doubles when Selina is turned on by the scheduling constraint ('wheels up at 9:30'), revealing her attraction to presidential authority even in her own sex life.
jokes.explanation: Two-beat joke with a surprising second layer. 'Do you want to just have sex?' is refreshingly blunt but the real laugh is 'Ooh, that turns me on' in response to a scheduling constraint — Selina is aroused by the trappings of presidential power even in the bedroom. Deep character integration and excellent economy. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Set a reminder. / Make an appointment to freeze eggs.
jokes.setup: Amy is rushing after Selina, doing multiple things at once.
jokes.punchline: Amy's reminder to herself to freeze her eggs — delivered as a casual administrative task in the midst of a political crisis — reveals the crushing personal cost of her career with characteristic dark humor.
jokes.explanation: A devastating five-word joke. The casualness with which Amy schedules egg freezing between political tasks is both very funny and quietly horrifying — a perfect encapsulation of what Selina's world costs the women who serve her. Exceptional economy (five words, maximum impact) and nearly perfect character integration. One of the episode's best jokes.

jokes.text: I want you to fire Gerry Duggan, Phil Neary and what's her ass, Lisa... / Hatch? / Yeah, I want her out of the comms department, okay? / Because they should not have let an article about me being called a cunt get out in the first place.
jokes.setup: Selina decides to respond to the Cuntgate investigation not by finding the leaker but by firing communications staff.
jokes.punchline: Selina fires three people for failing to suppress an article about her being called a cunt — not realizing she is creating more of exactly that problem by having people fired over the word 'cunt.'
jokes.explanation: The irony of Selina's response — firing people over the cunt story, thereby generating more cunt story — is clear but understated. 'What's her ass' is a fun Selina verbal tic. Solid escalation of the running plot.

jokes.text: I'll tell you something, Amy. A lot of people don't want me to be president. And you know why. Because fundamentally, people hate women, right?
jokes.setup: Selina is processing the indignity of being called a cunt.
jokes.punchline: Selina reaches for feminist grievance — 'people hate women' — in a context where the specific person who called her a cunt is Mike, who works for her and who she is also in the process of investigating. The grand societal claim rings hollow given the petty personal drama.
jokes.explanation: A sharp piece of political satire — Selina's genuine feminist grievance is real but also used as a convenient shield against self-reflection. The audience knows it was Mike (her loyal staffer) who called her that, making her political persecution narrative doubly ironic.

jokes.text: You okay, ma'am? / Have you ever been called a cunt? / Many times. / Well, now I have, too, apparently once.
jokes.setup: Selina is emotionally affected by the C-word story.
jokes.punchline: Amy's breezy 'many times' — delivered without any indication of trauma or self-pity — is the real joke, followed by Selina's deflated 'apparently once,' which reveals both her wounded pride and her belated membership in a club Amy has long belonged to.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best exchange. Amy's 'many times' — utterly matter-of-fact, not a moment of self-pity — is a perfectly compressed piece of character work that says volumes about what women in politics endure. Selina's 'apparently once' is even better: the 'apparently' hedges the insult she's already confirmed, and the 'once' establishes her wounded but competitive relationship to the indignity. Exceptional structure, economy, and character integration for both characters. Exceeds the 8.0 anchor benchmark.

jokes.text: Hey, Mom, I'm back. / You were gone? Oh, right, you were in New Hampshire.
jokes.setup: Catherine returns from New Hampshire and greets her mother.
jokes.punchline: Selina has no idea Catherine was even away — the half-remembered 'Oh, right, you were in New Hampshire' reveals the complete inattentiveness Selina has toward her own daughter.
jokes.explanation: A quiet but precise character joke. Selina's failure to track her own daughter's whereabouts is delivered without emphasis, which makes it funnier. The belated reconstruction 'Oh, right...' is very natural and specific.

jokes.text: Catherine's relationship backstory monologue: '...why things didn't work out between me and Jason. Or the guy in college who wanted to watch me pee. Or the guy with the weirdly high voice. No, there were two guys with weirdly high voices. / Yeah, there were two.'
jokes.setup: Catherine tries to talk to Selina about her relationship patterns, starting reasonably.
jokes.punchline: Catherine's list of relationship failures escalates to increasingly alarming specificity — the guy who wanted to watch her pee, then one then two guys with weirdly high voices — with Selina gamely confirming 'Yeah, there were two.'
jokes.explanation: An excellent escalation. Catherine's romantic history is painted in a few vivid strokes, each stranger than the last. Selina's total normalcy about confirming the 'two guys with weirdly high voices' — as though this is routine parenting knowledge — is the best part. Sets up the episode's payoff about Marjorie beautifully.

jokes.text: Hanukah polls higher in Mecca.
jokes.setup: Kent reveals the approval rating for bailing out Selina's boyfriend's bank is 4.3%.
jokes.punchline: Kent's statistical comparison — Hanukkah polling higher in Mecca than Selina's bank decision — is an absurdly precise analogy for something being the least popular thing imaginable in the least receptive context.
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best line. 'Hanukkah polls higher in Mecca' achieves extraordinary compression: it communicates 'this is maximally unpopular with a maximally unreceptive audience' with six words, using an analogy that is simultaneously absurd, culturally specific, and politically relevant. Kent's deadpan data-analyst delivery makes it perfect. This is the kind of line that becomes a Veep cultural touchstone — rivaling the 8.0 anchor and approaching the 9.5 anchor benchmark.

jokes.text: What would you guys do if you had to choose between your cock and your balls? / I could lose them both. I mean, at this stage they're purely decorative.
jokes.setup: Selina uses a crude anatomical metaphor to describe her dilemma about which bank to bail out.
jokes.punchline: Ben's response — that he'd lose both his cock and his balls, because 'at this stage they're purely decorative' — is a shockingly candid admission of his sexual irrelevance, delivered with total world-weary resignation.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy for Ben. His answer transcends the rhetorical question entirely and becomes a confessional moment about his own obsolescence. 'Purely decorative' is a beautifully precise and unexpected word choice for genitalia. Strong integration of Ben's exhausted, post-prime worldview.

jokes.text: Guys, you want to go out later for some Hansong Korean Barbecue? / Korean barbecue is a travesty and a far cry from authentic. / I'm his plus one.
jokes.setup: Mike casually suggests Korean barbecue to the group.
jokes.punchline: Sue delivers a harsh culinary verdict ('Korean barbecue is a travesty and a far cry from authentic'), and a third staffer immediately claims Sue as a plus-one — the Groupon from earlier is suddenly a group outing.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the Groupon and Korean barbecue is set up well earlier. Sue's food criticism is character-appropriate (her cultural standards are impeccable and cutting) and the 'I'm his plus one' is a quick, funny aside. The callback structure works.

jokes.text: That's Korean for 'I hate you.' / Marjorie? / That is her name, right?
jokes.setup: Someone is addressed as 'Sue' (possibly Marjorie or a mix-up is occurring).
jokes.punchline: Sue delivers 'That's Korean for I hate you' — apparently meaning her own name means 'I hate you' in Korean (or she is sardonically translating her mood). Selina's genuine uncertainty 'Marjorie? That is her name, right?' reveals she doesn't know her own Secret Service agent's name.
jokes.explanation: Sue's Korean name translation gag is delightfully deadpan and character-appropriate. Selina not knowing Marjorie's name (a setup for the episode's reveal) is a nice piece of dramatic irony in retrospect.

jokes.text: Thank nonexistent Jesus.
jokes.setup: After repeated reversals, Selina finally commits to bailing out Paulsten (not Charlie's bank).
jokes.punchline: A staffer's relief is expressed via an atheistic invocation — 'Thank nonexistent Jesus' — the specificity of 'nonexistent' making it funnier than a simple expletive.
jokes.explanation: A neat, economical joke. 'Thank nonexistent Jesus' is a funny atheist prayer format — the 'nonexistent' qualifier makes it specific and original rather than just a blasphemy. Good economy.

jokes.text: They make you look half smart, you fucking goon. / You know what we call people like you back in my day? / Retards.
jokes.setup: Amy and Dan argue about Jonah wearing glasses as part of his campaign relaunch.
jokes.punchline: Dan deploys a slur as vintage political wisdom — 'You know what we call people like you back in my day? Retards' — delivered as though this is a meaningful contribution to the glasses debate.
jokes.explanation: The joke is Dan's casual deployment of an offensive slur as retro-wisdom, which is in-character for his generational contempt, but it's a somewhat cheap laugh. The 'back in my day' framing is funny but the word itself is the main load-bearer, which leans cheap.

jokes.text: Tom Petty says we can't use 'Won't Back Down' anymore. / Okay, well, fuck him if he thinks I'm gonna back down. That's, like, the whole point of the song.
jokes.setup: Richard reports another preemptive cease-and-desist from a musician.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's response to Tom Petty's cease-and-desist about 'Won't Back Down' is to invoke the song's own lyrics as a philosophical argument for defiance — completely missing the legal/copyright reality.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Jonah using the lyrics of 'Won't Back Down' as a legal argument against Tom Petty's cease-and-desist about 'Won't Back Down' is a perfect closed loop of absurdist logic. The character integration is exceptional — only Jonah would think this way, and the structure of the joke (the song's message used as a response to the song's withdrawal) is impeccably constructed.

jokes.text: Also, sir, some bands we haven't used have sent some preemptive cease-and-desist orders. Sting, Bruce Springsteen... sent two letters, actually... and Enya. / Enya? / Send her a fucking cease-and-desist letter.
jokes.setup: Richard lists musicians who have preemptively objected to Jonah using their music.
jokes.punchline: The escalating prestige of artists (Sting, Springsteen — who is so emphatic he sent two letters) culminates in Enya — and Jonah's response to Enya's preemptive C&D is to send a counter-C&D to Enya.
jokes.explanation: The running gag about musicians rejecting Jonah peaks beautifully here. Enya's inclusion is hilarious — so ethereal, new-age, and politically non-threatening that her preemptive objection to Jonah is the ultimate indignity. Jonah's counter-C&D response to Enya is perfectly self-defeating and character-integrated. A strong episode highlight.

jokes.text: We got the okay from Gary Glitter, but he's in jail for child rape, so maybe not the first choice.
jokes.setup: Richard continues the musicians list, having failed to get permission from most artists.
jokes.punchline: Gary Glitter's availability is presented as a brief silver lining immediately negated — 'but he's in jail for child rape, so maybe not the first choice' — the 'maybe not the first choice' understating the problem magnificently.
jokes.explanation: 'Maybe not the first choice' for a convicted child rapist is a beautiful deployment of British understatement in an American political context. The casual presentation of this as a near-miss in the musicians search is darkly funny. The restraint of 'maybe not the first choice' is the joke's engine.

jokes.text: The widow's beating you by 30 points. / She's a retired second grade teacher, for Christ's sake. / Yeah, who sucks and gives too much homework. / She's your second grade teacher? / Yeah.
jokes.setup: Dan tries to motivate Jonah by pointing out he's losing to a retired teacher.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's complaint about the teacher 'giving too much homework' reveals he is running against his own actual second grade teacher, and still has childhood grievances against her.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character joke. The reveal that the widow teacher is Jonah's own second grade teacher — whom he's currently losing to — is structurally perfect. His complaint about 'too much homework' from decades ago applied to a congressional race is a devastating portrait of his arrested development. One of the episode's best character-comedy moments. Clearly surpasses the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: You suck! Go back to Washington. / I think she did a fine job screwing up the country all by herself.
jokes.setup: Jonah is heckled at a campaign event for his association with Selina.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's defense of himself requires him to throw Selina under the bus — 'She did a fine job screwing up the country all by herself' — simultaneously attacking his boss and inadvertently admitting his own uselessness.
jokes.explanation: A nice ironic moment — Jonah's self-exculpation accidentally works as both a political attack on Selina and a confirmation of his own irrelevance. Well-structured double meaning.

jokes.text: Bill. I thought you were in the... / The slammer? My lawyer got it thrown out on appeal. / O'Brien hired me to be the Widow Sherman's new campaign manager. / You're kidding me. / No, Dan. One thing you learn in prison is not to make jokes.
jokes.setup: Dan encounters Bill Ericsson (a previous antagonist) unexpectedly at the campaign event.
jokes.punchline: Bill's 'One thing you learn in prison is not to make jokes' — delivered straight — is simultaneously a joke, a threat, and a character reveal.
jokes.explanation: A tight, excellently structured joke. The setup is the 'You're kidding me' line, which Bill deflects with the prison wisdom about not joking — which is itself delivered as a joke, creating a lovely meta-irony. Economical and character-specific.

jokes.text: I am fructose intolerant, you nitwit.
jokes.setup: Someone (presumably Jonah or a staffer) has offered Bill something sugary or fruit-based.
jokes.punchline: Bill's irritated non-sequitur about fructose intolerance — delivered as his exit line — is a wonderfully abrupt deflation of the dramatic political confrontation.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — a dramatic villain re-entrance punctuated by a mundane dietary complaint. The word 'nitwit' is an unexpectedly formal insult from someone who just returned from prison. Economy score is very high because five words do enormous tonal work.

jokes.text: I assume what I'm seeing is the patented Egan touch. / I'll see you on the campaign trail, amigo. / Tell the president no hard feelings. / Oh, wait, that's right. I do have hard feelings. I'm consumed by them.
jokes.setup: Bill prepares to leave, offering a conventional political farewell.
jokes.punchline: Bill begins a gracious 'no hard feelings' exit then immediately reverses — 'Oh, wait, that's right. I do have hard feelings. I'm consumed by them.' — the self-correction arriving like a belated emotional audit.
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection joke. The gracious political farewell is set up as a genre convention, then Bill catches himself being conventionally polite and corrects it with absolute candor: 'I'm consumed by them.' The self-correction structure is original and the payoff is highly quotable. Meets the 8.0 anchor benchmark.

jokes.text: Oh, Catherine. Honey, you wanted to talk to me privately before. Now is a perfect time. / Really? / Mm-hmm. Now, what happened to the boyfriend with the high voice? / He couldn't go to the bathroom?
jokes.setup: Selina offers Catherine the private conversation she's been requesting — but only because Tom James has appeared and she wants an excuse to avoid him.
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'private mother-daughter time' is revealed as evasion the moment Tom leaves. Then her question about the high-voiced boyfriend — 'He couldn't go to the bathroom?' — is a hilariously specific and wrong-footed attempt to engage with Catherine's love life.
jokes.explanation: Selina's 'He couldn't go to the bathroom?' is a wonderful misdirection — suggesting she thinks the high voice was a urinary condition, or perhaps is confusing this relationship with the pee-voyeur boyfriend. Either way, it's a perfectly confused attempt at maternal engagement.

jokes.text: On second thought, we are now going to bail out Charlie and Wheelright. Thank you. / Yeah. / No. You know what? On third thought, let's just stick with the plan before. We're going to bail out Paulsten.
jokes.setup: Selina has been influenced by Tom James to change her mind (again) on the bank decision.
jokes.punchline: Selina reverses her decision in real-time, mid-announcement, then reverses back — ending with 'on third thought' as a concept, the absurdity of decision-making by pure impulse made explicit.
jokes.explanation: The comedy of Selina's decision paralysis has been building all episode, and 'on third thought' as a formalized concept is a nice structural payoff. The staffers' deadpan acceptance of the endless reversals adds to the joke.

jokes.text: Huh, you eat. / I suppose you need something to nervously shit out.
jokes.setup: Amy and Leon (a reporter) encounter each other. Leon notices Amy eating.
jokes.punchline: Amy's response to the observation that she's eating is 'I suppose you need something to nervously shit out' — a characteristically aggressive and self-aware piece of anxiety-management humor.
jokes.explanation: Amy's self-laceration is a consistent character note — she turns her anxiety into a weapon. 'Nervously shit out' is a vivid, original expression for stress-eating, and the matter-of-fact delivery is perfectly Amy.

jokes.text: Hello, Leon. It's always good to see the most left-swiped face on Tinder.
jokes.setup: Amy greets Leon, a reporter who has been covering the White House.
jokes.punchline: Amy's greeting is a Tinder-based insult — Leon is so unattractive he is swiped left by the maximum number of people — delivered as a cordial hello.
jokes.explanation: A sharp, specific, contemporary insult that works as a character joke for Amy (her aggression disguised as pleasantry). 'Most left-swiped' is a precise and original formulation. Well-constructed.

jokes.text: Or is a panicky mass firing because someone called POTUS a cunt in 'Politico'? / Ah, I just blew the lid off of Cuntgate.
jokes.setup: Leon probes Amy about the communications office firings.
jokes.punchline: Leon triumphantly declares he's 'blown the lid off Cuntgate' — a phrase that is itself the joke: 'Cuntgate' as a gate is absurd, and 'blew the lid off' applied to something so petty is a parody of investigative journalism self-importance.
jokes.explanation: Leon's self-congratulatory journalism speak applied to a scandal involving the word 'cunt' is a solid satirical joke. Part of the running 'is it a gate?' debate.

jokes.text: - It's not a gate. - No, it's very much a gate. / You should watch out, Amy. / You don't want to be the face of Cuntgate. / Although you do have the perfect face for it.
jokes.setup: Amy and Leon debate whether the C-word scandal qualifies as a '-gate.'
jokes.punchline: Leon warns Amy she doesn't want to be 'the face of Cuntgate' — then immediately adds 'although you do have the perfect face for it' — a devastating double-layered insult suggesting her face suits the word.
jokes.explanation: An outstanding two-step joke. Leon sets up what sounds like political advice ('you don't want to be the face of Cuntgate'), then the brutal addendum ('although you do have the perfect face for it') simultaneously roasts Amy's appearance AND implies her face looks like the word 'cunt.' Sharp, mean, perfectly economical — and the structure sets up and subverts in a single breath. Exceeds the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Good evening. It is me again. / Um, yeah, so I was just talking to Mike and I definitely want to bail out Wheelright.
jokes.setup: Selina calls her advisors again — for what is clearly the fifth or sixth position change on the bank decision.
jokes.punchline: The deadpan 'It is me again' — Selina's awareness that she keeps calling — combined with another reversal, reduces the presidential decision-making process to compulsive phone calls.
jokes.explanation: 'It is me again' is a beautifully self-aware acknowledgment of her own dysfunction, while not even slowing down the reversal. Strong running gag payoff.

jokes.text: Mike! You fucking jagoff. / Is this about the coffee pods? I swear I was gonna put... / No, you told the president to bail out Charlie's bank.
jokes.setup: Amy confronts Mike about influencing Selina's bank decision.
jokes.punchline: Mike's automatic assumption is again that he's in trouble about coffee pods — even after his earlier coffee pod confession — and begins to re-justify it.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the coffee pod guilt is excellent and pays off even better on second deployment — Mike's guilty conscience about coffee pods is now a recurring pathology rather than a one-off. 'I swear I was gonna put...' as an incomplete defense is perfect.

jokes.text: No, I didn't say anything about banks. / I was talking to her about how Wendy paid off my student loans and how I love her for it. / You still have student loans? How old are you? / I'm not good with money.
jokes.setup: Mike defends himself by explaining his conversation with Selina was personal, not about banks.
jokes.punchline: Mike's inadvertent bank influence came from talking about his wife paying off his student loans — which he still has at his age — revealed with total absence of shame by Mike's 'I'm not good with money.'
jokes.explanation: A nice character revelation — Mike's financial incompetence is shown not told, and 'I'm not good with money' as a matter-of-fact admission from a senior White House staffer is perfectly Mike. The Wendy detail (his wife paid his loans) adds an endearing/pathetic dimension.

jokes.text: I guess when he had cancer, the doctors removed his ability to stop doing his kid's babysitter.
jokes.setup: Dan coaches Jonah on his stump speech, suggesting a joke about Mayor Block.
jokes.punchline: The joke Jonah is given to tell is an extraordinarily mean-spirited joke about a cancer survivor's infidelity — connecting his illness to his inability to stop sleeping with the babysitter.
jokes.explanation: Dark and mean, but the surgery-infidelity connection is original. The show's willingness to go there is consistent with its satirical register. Dan's total confidence in this as a 'big laugh' is the real joke — his sociopathic campaign instincts presented as wisdom.

jokes.text: Good morning, Mrs. Sherman. / I still remember that lovely macaroni portrait you made of your mother. / I don't recall that macaroni portrait, Mrs. Sherman.
jokes.setup: Jonah and his opponent Mrs. Sherman — his actual second grade teacher — meet before speaking at the same event.
jokes.punchline: Mrs. Sherman needles Jonah with a childhood memory (the macaroni portrait) that Jonah has no recollection of — or perhaps is actively suppressing — while maintaining his formal tone.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to the second-grade teacher reveal. Mrs. Sherman's deployment of a childhood craft project as political warfare — 'that lovely macaroni portrait you made of your mother' — is subtle and devastating. Jonah's 'I don't recall that macaroni portrait' is a formal political non-denial that reveals both his discomfort and his inability to function outside political register even with a literal school ma'am.

jokes.text: Did you hear that dig about the macaroni portrait? / Can you fucking believe that?
jokes.setup: Jonah is furious after the Mrs. Sherman encounter.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's outrage that a macaroni portrait — a child's craft project — constitutes a political 'dig' reveals how deeply the former teacher's authority over him persists. He is more wounded by the macaroni portrait than by the 30-point polling gap.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's genuine grievance about a macaroni portrait is a perfect character comedy beat — his emotional arrested development made explicit. 'Can you fucking believe that?' about a childhood craft reference is wonderfully disproportionate.

jokes.text: Hey, slow down. I'm on Coumadin.
jokes.setup: The team rushes to reach Selina as she goes on another decision-making spree.
jokes.punchline: Ben's only objection to the crisis pace is that his blood thinner requires him to move carefully — reducing a political crisis to a geriatric scheduling constraint.
jokes.explanation: Ben's Coumadin (a blood thinner, commonly associated with elderly patients) becomes a perfect character shorthand for his physical and metaphorical running-out-of-steam. Four words, maximum character economy. 'I'm on Coumadin' as a political excuse is genuinely funny.

jokes.text: Sue, we need to see the president. / Gentlemen, you'll have to towel off and wait. / She's in with someone. / Who? / Whom.
jokes.setup: Ben and Kent rush to see Selina, apparently sweaty from rushing.
jokes.punchline: Sue's grammar correction — 'Whom' — in response to 'Who?' is both a pedantic joke and a perfect power move: Sue controls access to the president and will correct your grammar while making you wait.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes for economy and character integration. 'Whom' as Sue's only answer is perfect: it's simultaneously an answer (she won't tell them), a correction, and a power display. Sue's character — crisp, correct, gatekeeping — is entirely captured in one word. Exceptional economy. Meets the 8.0 anchor benchmark.

jokes.text: Gentlemen, you'll have to towel off and wait.
jokes.setup: Ben and Kent have been running to reach Selina and are apparently sweating.
jokes.punchline: Sue's instruction to 'towel off' — as though this is standard procedure for visiting the president — combined with her complete composure, is a quietly funny visual confirmation of their physical state.
jokes.explanation: Part of the same beat as joke 75 but worth noting as a distinct visual gag: Ben and Kent's disheveled, sweaty arrival is implied by Sue's instruction. The casualness of 'towel off' as a visiting protocol is funny.

jokes.text: Come on, Charlie, 100%. / 100%. / All right, great. / Now, on the other hand, bailing out your boyfriend, I mean, that could be career-ending, right? / Yeah, right. / But, I mean, if you had a gun to your head... / Oh, maybe the gun doesn't have any bullets.
jokes.setup: Tom James is ostensibly giving Selina objective advice about the bank bailout decision.
jokes.punchline: The two are clearly engaged in a mutual seduction/manipulation — agreeing with everything the other says — culminating in Tom's absurd 'maybe the gun doesn't have any bullets' as a way of not committing to any real advice.
jokes.explanation: Tom's complete uselessness as an advisor — offered through a series of mutual agreements and a metaphor that dissolves under its own weight ('gun without bullets') — is a solid character comedy beat. The scene is cringe-funny in its transparency.

jokes.text: If we don't put a plan into action tomorrow morning, then by Monday we will be Greece. / The country, not the musical.
jokes.setup: An advisor warns about the dire consequences of inaction on the bank crisis.
jokes.punchline: The clarification 'The country, not the musical' — specifying which Greece they mean — is absurd because nobody in a White House briefing would confuse them, but also funny because the effort to be clear implies someone might.
jokes.explanation: A clean, well-constructed joke. The Greece/Grease clarification is an unexpected left turn from the economic crisis gravity. 'The country, not the musical' has a high quotability because of its deadpan absurdity in context — a White House briefing clarifying 1970s movie musicals.

jokes.text: I got an idea. You can clear your head out and have a little fun at the same time. / What? / Yeah, I'll call Jonah and Dan and you can tear them a six-pack of new assholes.
jokes.setup: Ben offers Selina a stress-relief suggestion.
jokes.punchline: 'Tear them a six-pack of new assholes' — an escalation of the standard idiom 'tear them a new one' into a six-pack — is a Veep-specific hyperbolic intensifier that reveals both Ben's knowledge of Selina's psychology and the show's commitment to maximally profane originality.
jokes.explanation: The quantification of assholes — specifically six, a pack — is original and funny as an escalation of a familiar expression. The context (berating Jonah as presidential therapy) is also in-character for Selina. Meets the 8.0 anchor benchmark on craft.

jokes.text: Dan, I cannot fucking believe how terrible you are at your job. What, are you running on a platform of higher taxes and episiotomies?
jokes.setup: Selina calls Dan to berate him about Jonah's campaign performance.
jokes.punchline: 'Running on a platform of higher taxes and episiotomies' — combining two maximally unpopular things (taxes) with a very specific, painful medical procedure (episiotomies) as a rhetorical shorthand for a terrible political platform.
jokes.explanation: An excellent Selina insult. 'Higher taxes and episiotomies' as a campaign platform is a wildly original pairing — taxes (universal political poison) with episiotomies (a very specific, painful, and gendered medical procedure). The combination is unexpected, vivid, and deeply character-integrated for Selina's profane, high-octane verbal style. Quotability is very high. Clears the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Put that world's tallest pile of garbage on the phone right now. / It's the president. / Fuck... / Hello, ma'am. / Hey, hunchback.
jokes.setup: Selina demands to speak directly to Jonah.
jokes.punchline: The sequence of 'world's tallest pile of garbage' → 'hunchback' as successive nicknames for Jonah from the President of the United States is a beautifully degrading escalation. Jonah's 'Fuck...' before answering establishes his dread perfectly.
jokes.explanation: 'World's tallest pile of garbage' is an excellent, original, and image-specific insult for Jonah — combining his height with his worthlessness in one phrase. 'Hunchback' as the greeting is equally good. The sequence of insults building to the call connection is excellent escalation structure. Meets the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: I don't know what you've been doing instead of trying to win, but I'm gonna guess that it has the word anal in it.
jokes.setup: Selina continues berating Jonah on the phone.
jokes.punchline: The 'anal' reference — delivered as a presidential assessment of Jonah's activities — is crude but character-specific: Selina's contempt for Jonah often takes a sexualized form, and 'it has the word anal in it' is sufficiently vague to be technically deniable.
jokes.explanation: Good Selina-to-Jonah insult. The vagueness ('has the word anal in it') is funnier than a direct insult would be — it implies a range of activities and leaves Jonah to fill in the worst interpretation.

jokes.text: Now, you learn to control your cocksucking temper, otherwise I'm gonna come up there myself and I'm gonna shoot you in your fucking mouth.
jokes.setup: Selina's phone call to Jonah reaches its climax.
jokes.punchline: Selina's instruction for Jonah to 'control his cocksucking temper' — while making an explicit death threat about shooting him in the mouth — is a perfect piece of presidential irony: she cannot control her own temper while demanding he control his.
jokes.explanation: The irony structure is excellent: Selina's furious demand for anger management escalates to a literal death threat, making her the exemplar of the thing she's criticizing. 'Cocksucking temper' as a compound is also original. High character integration — this kind of violent rhetoric-while-demanding-calm is distinctly Selina.

jokes.text: She hung up. / Fuck her. / You know, Selina Meyer is a second-rate mediocrity whose only achievement is single-handedly tanking the economy. / But somehow we're the losers doing anal with each other? / Her entire presidency has been one disgrace after another. / She has never respected me. / And I'm bad at my fucking job? / Fuck! / You know what? That's bullshit. / You're doing a good job. / Oh, fuck off. / No, this glasses shit, that was dope. These look great. That was a great idea.
jokes.setup: After Selina hangs up on them, Jonah and Dan share a mutual venting session.
jokes.punchline: Dan and Jonah's mutual commiseration descends into Dan sincerely complimenting Jonah on the glasses ('that was dope') — the most begrudging and genuine bond these two have ever shared — Jonah telling him to fuck off, Dan insisting.
jokes.explanation: The sequence works because it's a genuine character moment: Dan and Jonah, two professional antagonists, bonding briefly over shared resentment. 'Doing anal with each other' as Jonah's metaphor for their working relationship, followed by Dan's sincere glasses compliment ('that was dope'), is a funny and oddly touching beat. The escalation from rage to begrudging respect is well-structured.

jokes.text: Yo, Granite State. Hey, how you feeling? / I see that Mayor Block isn't here tonight. Guess he couldn't get a babysitter. Or could he?
jokes.setup: Jonah delivers his stump speech including the mean babysitter joke Dan coached him on.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's delivery of the Mayor Block/babysitter joke — particularly the 'Or could he?' tag — turns what was a cruel-but-specific joke into a more open-ended one, making it somehow worse/better.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the coached joke pays off reasonably well. The 'Or could he?' improvisation by Jonah (or included in the script) is a nice touch — it somehow makes the joke more vague and therefore more offensive.

jokes.text: You suck! Go back to Washington. / But, yes, I was in Washington. / And I worked a very high-level, important job. / You helped Selina Meyer screw up this country. / I think she did a fine job screwing up the country all by herself.
jokes.setup: Jonah is heckled at the event about his connection to Selina.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's campaign strategy — distancing from Selina by throwing her under the bus — is executed in real-time, in public, at the event. 'I think she did a fine job screwing up the country all by herself' is simultaneously a political attack and a callback to Dan's strategic advice.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the strategy of attacking Selina through Jonah's own mouth has a satisfying structural symmetry. The improvised quality of the exchange — Jonah finding his feet through hecklers — is characteristically Jonah.

jokes.text: This is so much more beautiful to watch than the birth of my child.
jokes.setup: Dan watches Jonah's speech gain momentum through the heckler dynamic.
jokes.punchline: Dan's comparison of watching a successful campaign moment to childbirth — finding the former more emotionally resonant — is a perfectly dark statement of Dan's pathological career priorities.
jokes.explanation: An exceptional character joke. 'More beautiful than the birth of my child' is a perfectly Dan line — his utter devotion to political maneuvering over human connection, expressed without self-awareness or irony. The fact that he apparently has a child (easily forgotten or unmentioned) makes the indifference more shocking. High quotability, exceptional character integration. Surpasses the 8.0 anchor benchmark.

jokes.text: My name is Jonah Ryan and I want to be your congressman. And I won't back down. Thank you.
jokes.setup: Jonah closes his speech with a rallying cry.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's speech-closing 'I won't back down' — the title of the Tom Petty song whose cease-and-desist letter he received — is both a political slogan and an oblivious callback to his own copyright problem.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed callback. Jonah closing his speech with 'I won't back down' — the exact phrase/song he was told he couldn't use — is a perfect structural circle. His obliviousness is the joke: he's literally using the banned song's title as his slogan while not registering the irony. Excellent economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Can you confirm the allegation of the firing of three people from the comms office is tied to See-you-next-Tuesday-gate? / That sort of coarse language is far beneath the dignity of this room. / And also it's not a gate.
jokes.setup: A reporter asks about Cuntgate using the 'See-you-next-Tuesday' euphemism at the press briefing.
jokes.punchline: Mike's double response — first objecting to the euphemism's 'coarse language' (despite it being a euphemism) and then the secondary objection 'and also it's not a gate' — continues the running 'is it a gate' debate while revealing Mike's misplaced priorities.
jokes.explanation: Mike's two objections in order of priority — dignity first, then accuracy — is a funny portrait of his press secretary instincts. The 'See-you-next-Tuesday' euphemism being called coarse language is a nice absurdist inversion. Solid continuation of the running gag.

jokes.text: Ben, this is real. The whole room was sniffing around Cuntgate. / It's not a gate, Mike. / No kidding! It isn't a gate. I agree. I said that. / She's becoming seriously unhinged. / She has gone full metal Nixon.
jokes.setup: Mike debriefs Ben after the press briefing.
jokes.punchline: Mike's emphatic agreement that it's not a gate ('No kidding! It isn't a gate. I agree. I said that.') followed by Ben's clinical 'She has gone full metal Nixon' — collapsing two jokes into one continuous beat.
jokes.explanation: 'Full metal Nixon' as a descriptor for Selina's paranoid behavior is a well-chosen cultural reference that earns its place. The 'it's not a gate / No kidding / I agree / I said that' is funny as an overly emphatic agreement with oneself.

jokes.text: Who do you think said it? / Well, we did. Me and Mike. / Oh, my God. I thought it was me. / No way. / No, I'm pretty sure I called her a cunt to the reporter who broke the story.
jokes.setup: Ben and Amy discuss who called Selina a cunt.
jokes.punchline: The revelation escalates: Amy thought it was her, Ben confirms it was him AND Mike — and then Amy reveals she told the reporter who broke the story, meaning multiple people called Selina a cunt independently.
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation structure. The running gag about who called Selina a cunt reaches a perfect comedic climax: it wasn't one disloyal staffer — they ALL did it, independently, without coordination. The structure of competitive confession ('I thought it was me' / 'No, I'm pretty sure I called her a cunt to the reporter') is perfectly Veep. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Amy, it's probable that your investigation has already determined that I was the one that called her a cunt. / I'm hoping that my utility to the organization gives you reason to protect me on this. / Also our friendship.
jokes.setup: Ben realizes Amy's investigation points to him.
jokes.punchline: Ben's formal, corporate-register plea — 'my utility to the organization' — deployed as a reason for Amy to cover up his cunt-calling, followed by the bathetic 'Also our friendship' — the last-ditch appeal to a relationship that clearly doesn't supersede professional calculations.
jokes.explanation: Ben's political-organizational framing of personal accountability ('utility to the organization') followed by the deflated 'Also our friendship' is a beautifully constructed character joke. The two-part structure — formal professional argument, then personal appeal as a distant afterthought — perfectly captures Ben's world in which professional value is real currency and friendship is an addendum. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Gary. / It was me. I called the president the C word. / No, you didn't. / Really? / I was so mad about her not wearing sunscreen, which is stupid, and I was like, 'What an old crone.' / A what? / An old crone, Amy. / Gary, C is for cunt. / What? / We all called her a cunt. / Oh, my God! / What is wrong with you people?
jokes.setup: Gary arrives to confess that he is the one who called Selina the C word.
jokes.punchline: Gary's confession is immediately undercut by the revelation that he called her an 'old crone' — not 'cunt' — having misunderstood what the C word was in context. Amy and Ben's reaction ('We all called her a cunt / Oh, my God / What is wrong with you people?') is the perfect capper.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke and a genuine television comedy highlight. The structure is impeccable: Gary's noble self-sacrifice (he's confessing to protect Selina) is built up, then systematically dismantled by the revelation that he doesn't know what the C word is — he called her 'an old crone.' His earnest 'I was so mad about her not wearing sunscreen' as the context for this epic misfire is perfectly Gary. Then the triple reveal: (1) it's not even the right C word, (2) it was actually multiple people who said the real word, (3) Gary's punchline is moral outrage at THEM — 'What is wrong with you people?' This is a story construction joke of the highest order, resolving a running thread with maximum surprise, character specificity, and emotional satisfaction. Approaches the 9.5 anchor benchmark.

jokes.text: I've made my final decision. / I'm gonna bail out Charlie. / Madam President, you really don't have to make that decision yet. / I mean, you got a little bit more time. / I don't need more time.
jokes.setup: Having just resolved the investigation, Selina walks in with new resolve on the bank decision.
jokes.punchline: After an entire episode of demanding more time for the decision, Selina now declares she 'doesn't need more time' — while her staff, having finally adjusted to her chaos, now try to give her more time.
jokes.explanation: A nice structural reversal — Selina and her staff have switched positions on the time question. The irony of the staff now begging for time after an episode of craving a decision is sharp and well-earned.

jokes.text: Hey, ma'am, I know you made the right choice with Charlie because when you have found someone you love, who makes you feel special every time he talks to you and you can be happy just thinking about him, you know... / I mean, that's more important than anything, you know? / Yeah.
jokes.setup: Gary gives Selina an earnest speech about love to validate her bank decision.
jokes.punchline: Gary's speech about 'someone you love who makes you feel special' is transparently about his own devotion to Selina — not Selina's relationship with Charlie — played completely straight while Selina accepts the validation at face value.
jokes.explanation: An excellent piece of long-running character comedy. Gary's speech is heartfelt — about his love for Selina — but she takes it as being about Charlie. The gap between what Gary means and what Selina hears is both funny and quietly sad. Exceptional character integration for Gary's unrequited devotion.

jokes.text: Madam President, what will you be buying for Mr. Baird? / I don't want to spoil Charlie's surprise under the tree... but I'm going to be purchasing a book of poetry by one of my favorite African-American poets, Wanda Coleman. / And I've got my eye on a graphic novel with a very strong female Asian protagonist. / So, merry Chris... happy holidays to everybody, right? / Right. / And let's go shopping. Thank you. / Please don't get me a book for Christmas. / I already have a book.
jokes.setup: Reporters ask Selina about her Christmas shopping.
jokes.punchline: Selina's performative diversity-gift-giving (African-American poet, female Asian protagonist) is a perfect satirical snapshot of tokenistic political optics — compounded by someone deadpanning 'Please don't get me a book for Christmas. I already have a book.'
jokes.explanation: Selina's consciously diverse gift-buying as a political display is well-observed satire. The 'I already have a book' punchline — from someone presumably not invited to receive gifts — is a perfect non-sequitur deflation. Good episode snapshot of political performance anxiety.

jokes.text: No, no, that wasn't supposed to come now. / Hey, ma'am. Catherine keeps calling. Do you want to take it? / Uh, what? / No, just let it ring. Okay. / I don't know why she keeps calling.
jokes.setup: Selina is in the middle of something and Catherine keeps calling.
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'I don't know why she keeps calling' — moments before the episode's revelation about Catherine's relationship — is a piece of dramatic irony the audience will appreciate on rewatch, delivered with total Selina obliviousness.
jokes.explanation: Works as dramatic irony — Selina ignoring Catherine's calls, the content of which will be the episode's emotional climax, while casually dismissing them. Strong rewatch value.

jokes.text: I'm Chapter 11. Janders is buying my assets. I'm CEO of a fucking yard sale. / Hey, Charlie, you know what? / How could you fucking do this to me? / What do you mean? / 'Politico' was right about you. / No, Charlie... / Hey, you said it wouldn't affect... / I know you had an eye job.
jokes.setup: Charlie calls Selina enraged — she bailed out the wrong bank, leaving his bankrupt. He attacks her character.
jokes.punchline: In the middle of Charlie's righteous fury about the bailout destroying his bank, he delivers the trump card: 'I know you had an eye job.' — the cosmetic surgery secret, deployed as his final devastating weapon in the breakup.
jokes.explanation: A superb callback and episode payoff. The eye job secret — planted earlier with Gary and Selina's moisturizer cover story — is paid off perfectly in the context of a breakup. 'I know you had an eye job' as a billionaire's parting shot during a banking crisis is both perfectly petty and deeply funny. The structure is excellent: the high-stakes economic drama deflates to personal vanity. One of the episode's top jokes.

jokes.text: You made the right call on the banks, ma'am. / Well, you can thank 'When Gary Met Sally' for that one. / He gave me some sappy speech and made me realize what an idiot I was being.
jokes.setup: A staffer congratulates Selina on the bank decision.
jokes.punchline: Selina credits the decision to 'When Gary Met Sally' — a nickname for Gary's love speech — both a film reference joke and a confirmation that Gary's speech about his own love for Selina accidentally worked as political counsel.
jokes.explanation: 'When Gary Met Sally' is an excellent nickname — punning on 'When Harry Met Sally' while perfectly capturing the nature of Gary and Selina's relationship. The irony that Gary's inadvertent love confession became sound political advice is a satisfying structural payoff.

jokes.text: In further good news, ma'am, Jonah had a huge polling bump since he hit on the strategy of attacking you. / What? He's attacking me? / Yeah, and the voters, they love it. / Is that good? / It's really good.
jokes.setup: A staffer reports on Jonah's campaign progress.
jokes.punchline: Selina is told that Jonah's strategy of attacking her — the President — is working, and she tentatively accepts this as 'good news,' revealing her willingness to absorb personal attacks for political gain while being surprised that she has to.
jokes.explanation: A solid ironic beat — the political operatives have arranged a situation where attacking the president is good for the president's team. Selina's 'Is that good?' is a funny moment of self-confusion.

jokes.text: Mom, there's something that I really need to find a way to say to you. / Catherine, I'm in the middle of a shitstorm like... / Please, I've been trying to talk to you for three days and I need to tell you something. / What is it, Catherine? What is it that is so pressing? / I've met someone and I know that this is awkward because you work with them, but we're in love. / Who? / Me and Marjorie. / Who? / Me, ma'am. / What?
jokes.setup: Catherine finally gets her chance to tell Selina about her relationship.
jokes.punchline: The reveal is layered: 'I've met someone' → 'you work with them' → 'Me and Marjorie' → Selina's 'Who?' → 'Me, ma'am' (Marjorie speaks up) → Selina's 'What?' Each beat of incomprehension from Selina is a fresh joke.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed scene. The multiple 'Who?' responses — Selina asking who Marjorie is, then Marjorie introducing herself — escalate through genuine surprise and Selina's total inattentiveness (she doesn't know Marjorie's name; see joke 50). The punchline structure is impeccable: each new 'Who?' is a fresh layer of Selina's obliviousness. Character-integrated throughout, with Selina's self-absorption, Catherine's long-suppressed announcement, and Marjorie's formal precision all working together.

jokes.text: Now you know why I've been acting all silly and giggly. / Didn't guess. Didn't guess. / Amazing. / It's... amazing.
jokes.setup: Catherine explains her giddiness now that the revelation is out.
jokes.punchline: Selina's shell-shocked 'Didn't guess. Didn't guess.' followed by two uses of 'amazing' — the second clearly not meaning wonderful but rather indicating bewildered processing — is a perfect comedic landing.
jokes.explanation: Selina's 'Didn't guess. Didn't guess.' is a masterclass in reaction comedy — the repetition reveals the depth of her shock, and the doubled structure (twice 'didn't guess') suggests she's trying to convince herself she's not horrified. The two 'amazings' with their shifting registers (genuinely attempting warmth, landing on stunned) are excellent character acting translated to page. High quotability.

jokes.text: Uh... / What... / I wish Mother were alive. / 'Cause this definitely would have killed her. / You know how she was.
jokes.setup: After Catherine and Marjorie leave, Selina processes the revelation.
jokes.punchline: Selina's only coherent thought is wishing her mother were alive — but not for emotional support: because 'this definitely would have killed her.' Her homophobic mother's death-by-revelation would have been satisfying.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's two or three best jokes. The structure is exceptional: the sentimental setup ('I wish Mother were alive') is immediately subverted by the punchline ('because this would have killed her'), and Gary's 'You know how she was' confirms the mother's known bigotry as shared knowledge. It's dark, original, economical, and perfectly character-integrated for Selina's complicated relationship with her own moral compass. The grandmother's established character (Mee-Maw) makes this a callback to the wider season. Approaches the 9.5 anchor.

jokes.text: Ma'am, is this a good time? / You bet. / The investigation continues at a rigorous pace. / So who called me a cunt? / Uh... / Was it everybody? / Pretty much, yeah. / You can go.
jokes.setup: Amy arrives to update Selina on the Cuntgate investigation.
jokes.punchline: Selina's immediate intuition — 'Was it everybody?' — is confirmed by Amy's 'Pretty much, yeah.' Selina's 'You can go' — accepting the verdict without dramatics — is the perfect anti-climactic resolution to the episode's central investigation.
jokes.explanation: The perfect resolution to the episode's A-plot. Selina's intuition ('Was it everybody?') is exactly right and delivered without self-pity, and Amy's 'Pretty much, yeah' is the ideal deadpan confirmation. 'You can go' as the closing statement — Selina processing the total betrayal of her staff and just... dismissing it — is peak Veep characterization: pragmatic, hollow, indomitable. Exceptional structure and economy. One of the top three jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Hi, called ahead for $100 worth of food to go. Under McLintock. / And I'll be using a Groupon. / We don't take Groupon. / You have to take Groupon. / I already paid money for this Groupon, sir. / No Groupon. Total is $184.12. / Whoa, I told the guy on the phone $100 worth of food. Where's this $89 coming from? / That's your problem, sir.
jokes.setup: Mike finally goes to Hansong Korean Barbecue — alone, with his Groupon.
jokes.punchline: The Groupon is not accepted, the total doesn't match what he was quoted, and Mike's helpless 'That's your problem, sir' defeat is the comic resolution to the episode's Groupon thread.
jokes.explanation: An excellent cold-ending runner. The Groupon has been mentioned three times; its resolution — Mike alone in Annandale, rejected, $84 over budget — is a perfect anti-climax that also functions as a character epitaph for Mike. The restaurant worker's 'That's your problem, sir' is perfectly deadpan. Well-constructed callback payoff. Meets the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Shit! Oh, my God. Oh, my God. / No, no, no, no.
jokes.setup: Mike has just been told the Groupon isn't accepted and the total is unexpectedly high.
jokes.punchline: Mike's escalating physical and verbal distress — 'Oh, my God. Oh, my God. No, no, no, no.' — suggests something has gone catastrophically wrong (he's dropped his food, or his card has declined) as the final image of the episode for this thread.
jokes.explanation: Mike's off-screen disaster (implied from his reaction) is a funny final beat — the audience fills in the physical comedy. Character-integrated as the culmination of Mike's hapless day. Solid physical/reaction comedy ending.

jokes.text: I can't stop smiling. / Neither can I.
jokes.setup: The episode ends on Catherine and Marjorie together.
jokes.punchline: Their genuine happiness — 'I can't stop smiling / Neither can I' — cuts against everything else in the episode (economic crisis, political chaos, personal betrayals) as the only moment of sincere contentment, and the final irony is that this is the thing Selina could least process.
jokes.explanation: A warm tag that functions as an ironic counterpoint to the episode's cynicism. Their happiness is the punchline to the episode's emotional arc — the thing Selina can't fix or control. Not a big laugh but a satisfying tonal note.

tmdb_overview: With the U.S. on the brink of a major financial meltdown, Selina must make an important decision. Jonah finds himself back in New Hampshire. Meanwhile, Amy investigates the staff, and Catherine tries to get time with Selina to share some major news.
insight: Political chaos spirals into profanity scandal; 73 jokes land at steady 2.07-per-minute clip.
